## [Ralph] [PLAN] Round 1 | Step: planning
**Time**: 2026-03-30 22:41:52

[PLAN]

## Task Goal
Implement three requirements to fix ImportErrors blocking 103 tests:
1. Add `streamable_http_client` + deprecation warnings in streamable_http.py
2. Add `CreateMessageResultWithTools` to types.py
3. Add `ClientCredentialsOAuthProvider`, `PrivateKeyJWTOAuthProvider`, `SignedJWTParameters`, `static_assertion_provider` to client_credentials.py

## Proposed Approach

### Requirement 1: streamable_http.py
- Add `streamable_http_client` as a new async context manager identical to `streamablehttp_client`
- Make `streamablehttp_client` emit `DeprecationWarning("Use \`streamable_http_client\` instead")` before yielding
- Modify `StreamableHTTPTransport.__init__` to emit `DeprecationWarning` when deprecated params (`headers`, `timeout`, `sse_read_timeout`, `auth`) are passed, and ignore their values
- Add `_prepare_headers(self) -> dict[str, str]` method returning `{"accept": "application/json, text/event-stream", "content-type": "application/json"}` (no deprecated headers)
- The new `streamable_http_client` will take `url` as the only positional arg and create the transport with only `url`

**Implementation detail**: The current `StreamableHTTPTransport.__init__` uses `headers`, `timeout`, `sse_read_timeout`, `auth` actively. The new `streamable_http_client` must continue working, so:
- Change `__init__` to only require `url`, make other params deprecated
- The new `streamable_http_client` will pass params directly to `httpx_client_factory` instead of through the transport constructor
- Actually, looking more carefully at the test: it constructs `StreamableHTTPTransport(url=..., headers=..., timeout=..., sse_read_timeout=..., auth=...)` and expects a DeprecationWarning, then `_prepare_headers()` should NOT include the deprecated headers. This means `__init__` should still accept these params but warn and ignore them.
- The existing `streamablehttp_client` calls `StreamableHTTPTransport(url, headers, timeout, sse_read_timeout, auth)` — this would trigger the deprecation warning. The new `streamable_http_client` should pass params differently (not through the constructor).

**Refined approach**: 
- `StreamableHTTPTransport.__init__` keeps `url` as required, keeps other params as optional with defaults, but when they're passed with non-default values, it emits a deprecation warning and ignores them
- Add `_prepare_headers()` returning standard headers dict
- The new `streamable_http_client` function creates transport with only `url`, then uses `timeout`/`sse_read_timeout`/`auth`/`headers` directly when creating the httpx client

### Requirement 2: types.py
- Add `CreateMessageResultWithTools = CreateMessageResult` (TypeAlias) at module scope
- This works because `CreateMessageResult` already has `content_as_list` property and accepts `ToolUseContent` as content (via `SamplingMessageContentBlock` union)
- Verify the existing `content` field already accepts `ToolUseContent` — yes, `SamplingMessageContentBlock` includes `ToolUseContent`

### Requirement 3: client_credentials.py
- Add `static_assertion_provider(token: str)` — returns async callable ignoring audience, returning token
- Add `SignedJWTParameters(BaseModel)` — fields: `issuer`, `subject`, `signing_key`, `signing_algorithm="RS256"`, `lifetime_seconds=300`, `additional_claims=None`. Method `create_assertion_provider()` returns async callable generating signed JWTs
- Add `ClientCredentialsOAuthProvider(OAuthClientProvider)` — simplified constructor taking `server_url`, `storage`, `client_id`, `client_secret`, `scopes=None`, `token_endpoint_auth_method="client_secret_basic"`. Override `_initialize` to set `client_info`. Override `_perform_authorization` to build client_credentials token request
- Add `PrivateKeyJWTOAuthProvider(OAuthClientProvider)` — constructor takes `server_url`, `storage`, `client_id`, `assertion_provider`, `scopes=None`. Override `_initialize` and `_perform_authorization`

Both credential providers need to pass a dummy `client_metadata` to the base `OAuthClientProvider.__init__` (which requires it). They'll create default `OAuthClientMetadata` internally.

## Expected Deliverables
- Modified `src/mcp/client/streamable_http.py`
- Modified `src/mcp/types.py`
- Modified `src/mcp/client/auth/extensions/client_credentials.py`

requirements.description:
The parameter `request_read_timeout_seconds` in `send_request` is typed as
      `timedelta | None`, but callers (including tests and internal call sites such as
      `ClientSession.call_tool`) pass plain `float` values (e.g. `0.5`, `0.000001`).
      At runtime the code unconditionally calls `.total_seconds()` on this parameter
      (line 286), which raises `AttributeError: 'float' object has no attribute
      'total_seconds'` whenever a float is supplied. This causes at least two test
      failures: `test_response_id_non_numeric_string_no_match` and
      `test_notification_validation_error`.
requirements.contract:
`send_request` must accept `timedelta | float | None` for
      `request_read_timeout_seconds`. When the value is a `timedelta`, extract
      seconds via `.total_seconds()`; when it is a plain `float`, use it directly
      as the number of seconds. The resulting numeric timeout is then passed to
      `anyio.fail_after`. The public method signature of `call_tool` in
      `ClientSession` must also be updated to declare `read_timeout_seconds:
      timedelta | float | None`.
requirements.acceptance_tests:
tests/shared/test_session.py::test_response_id_non_numeric_string_no_match
      tests/issues/test_88_random_error.py::test_notification_validation_error

requirements.description:
When a server returns a JSON-RPC response whose `id` is a string that
      represents the same integer as the original request ID (e.g. the client sent
      `id: 0` as an integer, but the server replies with `id: "0"` as a string),
      `_handle_response` performs a plain dict lookup (`self._response_streams.pop
      (response_id, None)`) which finds no match because `0 != "0"` in Python.
      The response is discarded and the waiting coroutine times out. The same
      mismatch occurs for error responses routed via `JSONRPCError`. Two tests
      expose this: `test_response_id_type_mismatch_string_to_int` and
      `test_error_response_id_type_mismatch_string_to_int`.
requirements.contract:
In `_handle_response`, after extracting `response_id` from the incoming
      message, apply a normalization step before looking up the pending stream:
      if `response_id` is a string whose content is a valid decimal integer (i.e.
      `str.isdigit()` or equivalent check), convert it to `int` before performing
      the lookup. Non-numeric string IDs must NOT be converted and must continue to
      result in a no-match (so that `test_response_id_non_numeric_string_no_match`
      stays passing). The same normalization must be applied when routing to
      `_response_routers` for consistency.
requirements.acceptance_tests:
tests/shared/test_session.py::test_response_id_type_mismatch_string_to_int
      tests/shared/test_session.py::test_error_response_id_type_mismatch_string_to_int

requirements.description:
The `mime_type` field on `Resource` is validated against the regex pattern
      `'^[a-zA-Z0-9]+/[a-zA-Z0-9\\-+.]+$'`. This pattern rejects any MIME type
      that includes parameters after a semicolon (e.g. `text/html;profile=mcp-app`,
      `text/plain; charset=utf-8`), which are valid per RFC 2045. As a result,
      decorating a resource function with a parameterized MIME type raises a
      `pydantic_core.ValidationError` before any user code runs. All four tests in
      `tests/issues/test_1754_mime_type_parameters.py` fail with this error.
requirements.contract:
The `pattern` constraint on `mime_type` must be relaxed to allow an optional
      parameters section consisting of a semicolon followed by one or more
      `token=token` pairs (with optional whitespace around the semicolon). At
      minimum the updated pattern must accept:
      - `text/plain` (no parameters, existing behaviour)
      - `text/html;profile=mcp-app` (parameter, no space)
      - `text/html; charset=utf-8` (parameter, with space)
      - `application/json+ld; version=1; charset=utf-8` (multiple parameters)
      The type annotation remains `str`; only the Pydantic `pattern` constraint
      needs changing.
requirements.acceptance_tests:
tests/issues/test_1754_mime_type_parameters.py::test_mime_type_with_parameters
      tests/issues/test_1754_mime_type_parameters.py::test_mime_type_with_parameters_and_space
      tests/issues/test_1754_mime_type_parameters.py::test_mime_type_with_multiple_parameters
      tests/issues/test_1754_mime_type_parameters.py::test_mime_type_preserved_in_read_resource

requirements.description:
`FastMCP.__init__` stores the caller-supplied `transport_security` value
      directly into `self.settings.transport_security` without any default
      initialization. When no `transport_security` argument is provided (the common
      case), the field remains `None` even for localhost/loopback hosts where DNS
      rebinding attacks are a real risk. Three tests assert that constructing
      `FastMCP` with `host="127.0.0.1"`, `host="localhost"`, or `host="::1"`
      automatically populates `self.settings.transport_security` with a
      `TransportSecuritySettings` object that has `enable_dns_rebinding_protection
      = True` and the appropriate entries in `allowed_hosts` and `allowed_origins`.
requirements.contract:
After `self.settings` is initialized inside `FastMCP.__init__`, if
      `transport_security` was `None` (caller did not provide explicit settings)
      AND the effective `host` value is one of the loopback addresses
      (`"127.0.0.1"`, `"localhost"`, `"::1"`), then `self.settings.transport_security`
      must be set to a newly-created `TransportSecuritySettings` instance with:
      - `enable_dns_rebinding_protection = True`
      - For `"127.0.0.1"` or `"localhost"`: `allowed_hosts` includes at least
        `"127.0.0.1:*"` and `"localhost:*"`; `allowed_origins` includes at least
        `"http://127.0.0.1:*"` and `"http://localhost:*"`.
      - For `"::1"`: `allowed_hosts` includes at least `"[::1]:*"`;
        `allowed_origins` includes at least `"http://[::1]:*"`.
      When the caller explicitly passes `transport_security` (even with
      `enable_dns_rebinding_protection=False`), that value must be used as-is
      without override. When `host` is anything other than the three loopback
      values above (e.g. `"0.0.0.0"`), `transport_security` must remain `None`.
requirements.acceptance_tests:
tests/server/fastmcp/test_server.py::TestDnsRebindingProtection::test_auto_enabled_for_127_0_0_1
      tests/server/fastmcp/test_server.py::TestDnsRebindingProtection::test_auto_enabled_for_localhost
      tests/server/fastmcp/test_server.py::TestDnsRebindingProtection::test_auto_enabled_for_ipv6_localhost
      tests/server/fastmcp/test_server.py::TestDnsRebindingProtection::test_not_auto_enabled_for_other_hosts
      tests/server/fastmcp/test_server.py::TestDnsRebindingProtection::test_explicit_settings_not_overridden

requirements.description:
`ClientSession.__init__` does not accept a `sampling_capabilities` keyword
      argument. The test `test_client_capabilities_with_sampling_tools` constructs
      a `ClientSession` with
      `sampling_capabilities=types.SamplingCapability(tools=types.SamplingToolsCapability())`
      and expects that the `initialize` handshake advertises those tool capabilities
      to the server. Currently the constructor raises
      `TypeError: ClientSession.__init__() got an unexpected keyword argument
      'sampling_capabilities'`.
requirements.contract:
Add an optional `sampling_capabilities: types.SamplingCapability | None = None`
      parameter to `ClientSession.__init__`. When provided, this value must be used
      as the `sampling` field of the `ClientCapabilities` sent during `initialize`
      (instead of the default `types.SamplingCapability()` that is created when a
      non-default `sampling_callback` is present). Specifically:
      - If `sampling_capabilities` is supplied (not None), use it as the
        `sampling` capability regardless of whether `sampling_callback` was also
        customized.
      - If `sampling_capabilities` is None and a custom `sampling_callback` was
        provided, fall back to the existing behaviour (create a bare
        `types.SamplingCapability()`).
      - If both are None/default, `sampling` remains None in the capabilities.
      The `SamplingCapability` type already has an optional `tools` field of type
      `SamplingToolsCapability | None`; no changes to types are required.
requirements.acceptance_tests: tests/client/test_session.py::test_client_capabilities_with_sampling_tools

requirements.location: code/src/mcp/server/auth/handlers/token.py — TokenHandler.handle() method, the except AuthenticationError block (currently around line 96–108)
requirements.description:
When ClientAuthenticator.authenticate_request() raises AuthenticationError (e.g., missing
      Authorization header, invalid Base64 encoding, client ID mismatch, wrong client_secret,
      or unsupported auth method), the handler currently returns a 401 response with
      error="unauthorized_client". Per RFC 6749 Section 5.2, "unauthorized_client" means the
      authenticated client is not authorized for the requested grant type — a fundamentally
      different condition. Authentication failures (bad credentials, unknown client) MUST use
      error="invalid_client". This incorrect error code causes 7 tests to fail.
requirements.contract:
- When an AuthenticationError is caught inside TokenHandler.handle(), the JSON response
        body MUST use error="invalid_client" (not "unauthorized_client").
      - The HTTP status code MUST remain 401.
      - The error_description field MUST still be populated from the AuthenticationError message.
      - No other behavior in the handler should change.
      - This aligns with RFC 6749 Section 5.2 semantics:
          * invalid_client   → Client authentication failed (credentials missing, invalid, or unknown)
          * unauthorized_client → Authenticated client is not permitted to use this grant type
requirements.acceptance_tests:
tests/server/fastmcp/auth/test_auth_integration.py::TestAuthEndpoints::test_token_invalid_client_secret_returns_invalid_client
      tests/server/fastmcp/auth/test_auth_integration.py::TestAuthEndpoints::test_wrong_auth_method_without_valid_credentials_fails
      tests/server/fastmcp/auth/test_auth_integration.py::TestAuthEndpoints::test_basic_auth_without_header_fails

      tests/server/fastmcp/auth/test_auth_integration.py::TestAuthEndpoints::test_basic_auth_client_id_mismatch_fails
      tests/server/fastmcp/auth/test_auth_integration.py::TestAuthEndpoints::test_token_validation_error

requirements.location: code/src/mcp/server/fastmcp/tools/base.py — Tool.run() async method (the except Exception block at the bottom, currently around lines 111–112)
requirements.description:
The Tool.run() method wraps all exceptions from fn_metadata.call_fn_with_arg_validation()
      with a generic "except Exception as e: raise ToolError(...) from e". This swallows McpError
      subclasses (including UrlElicitationRequiredError) and prevents them from propagating to the
      lowlevel server's request dispatcher, which has a dedicated "except McpError as err" handler
      that converts them into proper MCP error responses. As a result, a tool that raises
      UrlElicitationRequiredError does NOT cause the client to receive McpError; instead, it
      receives a ToolError-based error result, and pytest.raises(McpError) does not trigger.
requirements.contract:
- If the tool function raises an instance of McpError (or any subclass, including
        UrlElicitationRequiredError), Tool.run() MUST re-raise it unchanged, without wrapping it
        in ToolError.
      - Only non-McpError exceptions should be caught and wrapped with ToolError as before.
      - The McpError re-raise must happen before the generic except Exception handler so that
        the exception reaches the lowlevel server dispatcher intact.
      - The client must ultimately receive an MCP error response whose error.code equals
        types.URL_ELICITATION_REQUIRED and error.data["elicitations"] contains the expected list
        when UrlElicitationRequiredError is raised.
      - Normal exceptions (e.g., ValueError, RuntimeError) must still be caught and converted
        to ToolError as before.
requirements.acceptance_tests:
tests/server/fastmcp/test_url_elicitation_error_throw.py::test_url_elicitation_error_thrown_from_tool
      tests/server/fastmcp/test_url_elicitation_error_throw.py::test_url_elicitation_error_from_error

The module currently imports the streamable HTTP client factory as "streamablehttp_client"
      (no underscore between "http" and "client"). However, the test patches the attribute
      "mcp.client.session_group.streamable_http_client" (with underscore). Because that name
      does not exist in the module namespace, unittest.mock.patch raises AttributeError and the

requirements.contract:

        mcp.client.session_group and must refer to the same callable as the existing
        streamablehttp_client (i.e., the streamable HTTP client context-manager factory).
      - This can be achieved either by renaming the import alias or by adding an explicit alias
        at module level (e.g., streamable_http_client = streamablehttp_client).
      - All existing internal usages of streamablehttp_client within the module must continue
        to work correctly after this change.

        to that model are required.

requirements.location:
code/src/mcp/server/streamable_http.py — message_router() nested async function inside
      the connect() context manager (the except Exception block at the end of the try, around
      line 1008)
requirements.description:
When the write_stream_reader is closed during normal session teardown (e.g., when the
      session ends and streams are cleaned up), anyio.ClosedResourceError is raised inside the
      "async for session_message in write_stream_reader" loop. This is caught by the broad
      "except Exception: logger.exception('Error in message router')" handler, which emits an
      ERROR-level log entry. Test test_race_condition_message_router_async_for captures log
      records and fails when it finds any ERROR-level log containing "Error in message router".
requirements.contract:
- anyio.ClosedResourceError raised from iterating write_stream_reader MUST be treated as
        a normal shutdown signal: the message_router coroutine should exit cleanly without
        logging at ERROR level.
      - The fix must be applied so that ClosedResourceError is explicitly handled before the
        generic except Exception handler. It is acceptable to log at DEBUG level or to silently
        return/break from the loop.
      - Other unexpected exceptions (not ClosedResourceError) should still be caught and logged
        at ERROR level (via logger.exception) as before.
      - The behavior of the message_router for all normal message-routing operations must not
        change.
requirements.acceptance_tests: tests/issues/test_1363_race_condition_streamable_http.py::test_race_condition_message_router_async_for

requirements.location: src/mcp/client/sse.py — function `sse_reader` (nested inside `sse_client`), within the `case "message"` branch, approximately line 107–119.
requirements.description: The SSE reader currently attempts to parse every "message" event's `data` field as JSON unconditionally. When an SSE event carries an empty `data` field (i.e., `sse.data == ""`), `types.JSONRPCMessage.model_validate_json("")` raises a `ValidationError`. This exception is caught by the `except Exception` block and forwarded into the read stream, causing consumers to receive an `Exception` object instead of a valid message. The test asserts that empty-data "message" events (keep-alive pings per the MCP spec) must be silently skipped, but the current code does not guard against this case.
requirements.contract: Before attempting JSON parsing within the `case "message"` branch, the implementation must check whether `sse.data` is empty (falsy / zero-length string). If `sse.data` is empty, the event must be silently skipped (via `continue`) without writing anything to the read stream and without logging an error. Only non-empty `sse.data` values should be parsed and forwarded. All other behavior (parsing valid JSON, forwarding parsed messages, error-handling for genuinely malformed JSON) must remain unchanged.
requirements.acceptance_tests: tests/shared/test_sse.py::test_sse_client_handles_empty_keepalive_pings

requirements.description: The `Context.log()` method currently accepts only `level`, `message`, and `logger_name` parameters. The test calls it with an additional keyword argument `extra` (a dict of supplementary structured data). Because `extra` is not declared in the signature, the call raises `TypeError: Context.log() got an unexpected keyword argument 'extra'`, which causes the tool invocation to return `isError=True`. Furthermore, when `extra` is provided, the test expects the logging notification's `data` field to be a dict of the form `{"message": <message>, **extra}` rather than the plain string `<message>`.
requirements.contract:
The `Context.log()` signature must be extended to accept an optional keyword-only parameter `extra` of type `dict[str, Any] | None`, defaulting to `None`.

      Behavioral contract:
      - When `extra` is `None` (or not provided): behavior is identical to the current implementation — `data` passed to `send_log_message` is the plain `message` string.
      - When `extra` is a non-None dict: `data` passed to `send_log_message` must be a dict constructed as `{"message": message, **extra}`.

      The `level`, `logger_name`, and `related_request_id` arguments forwarded to `send_log_message` must remain unchanged in both cases.
requirements.acceptance_tests:
tests/client/test_logging_callback.py::test_logging_callback
      — specifically the assertions at lines 104–120, which verify:
        (a) `log_result_with_extra.isError is False`
        (b) `log_with_extra.data == {"message": "Test log message", "extra_string": "example", "extra_dict": {"a": 1, "b": 2, "c": 3}}`

requirements.description: The `_handle_token_response` method treats any status code other than exactly `200` as a failure, unconditionally raising `OAuthTokenError`. The test `test_token_exchange_accepts_201_status` sends a token response with HTTP status `201 Created`, which is a valid success response per the OAuth 2.0 / HTTP specification for resource-creation endpoints. The current strict equality check `if response.status_code != 200` rejects this valid response and raises an error, causing the OAuth flow to fail when the authorization server returns `201`.
requirements.contract: The success condition in `_handle_token_response` must be broadened to accept any HTTP 2xx status code (i.e., `200 <= response.status_code <= 299`) as a successful token response. Any status code outside this range must still raise `OAuthTokenError` with the existing error message format. All downstream behavior (parsing the token response body, storing tokens, updating token expiry) must remain unchanged for successful responses.
requirements.acceptance_tests:
tests/client/test_auth.py::TestAuthFlow::test_token_exchange_accepts_201_status
      — specifically the assertion at line 1178 that `final_request.headers["Authorization"] == "Bearer new_access_token"`, confirming the OAuth flow completes successfully when token endpoint returns HTTP 201.

requirements.location: src/mcp/client/streamable_http.py — module level and class StreamableHTTPTransport
requirements.description:
The module exports a function named `streamablehttp_client` (no underscore between "http" and
      "client"), but all importing test modules expect `streamable_http_client` (with underscore).
      This causes an ImportError that prevents collection of:
        - tests/client/test_http_unicode.py (2 tests)
        - tests/client/test_notification_response.py (1 test)
        - tests/server/fastmcp/test_integration.py (20 tests)
        - tests/shared/test_streamable_http.py (51 tests)
      Additionally, tests in test_streamable_http.py verify that:
        (a) `streamablehttp_client` emits a DeprecationWarning with message
            "Use `streamable_http_client` instead";
        (b) `StreamableHTTPTransport`, when instantiated with the deprecated positional/keyword
            parameters (`headers`, `timeout`, `sse_read_timeout`, `auth`), emits a
            DeprecationWarning and ignores those values;
        (c) `StreamableHTTPTransport` exposes a `_prepare_headers()` method (no arguments) that
            returns the standard request headers dict (with "accept" and "content-type" keys)
            without any headers that may have been passed through the deprecated constructor params.
requirements.contract:
1. Add a new async context-manager function `streamable_http_client` at module scope in
         `src/mcp/client/streamable_http.py`.  Its signature and behaviour must be identical to the
         existing `streamablehttp_client`: it accepts `url`, optional `headers`, `timeout`,
         `sse_read_timeout`, `terminate_on_close`, `httpx_client_factory`, and `auth` parameters
         and yields the same three-tuple
         `(read_stream, write_stream, get_session_id_callback)`.

      2. The existing `streamablehttp_client` function must be preserved but must emit a
         `DeprecationWarning` with the message "Use `streamable_http_client` instead" at call
         time (before yielding).

      3. `StreamableHTTPTransport.__init__` must continue to accept the old keyword arguments
         (`headers`, `timeout`, `sse_read_timeout`, `auth`) for backwards compatibility, but
         must emit a `DeprecationWarning` when any of those deprecated parameters is provided.
         The values of the deprecated parameters must NOT be applied to the instance's active
         request headers.

      4. `StreamableHTTPTransport` must expose a public method `_prepare_headers(self) ->
         dict[str, str]` (no additional arguments) that returns the standard transport headers
         containing at minimum:
           - "accept": "application/json, text/event-stream"
           - "content-type": "application/json"
         The returned dict must NOT contain any key-value pairs that were passed through the
         deprecated `headers` constructor parameter.

      Both `streamable_http_client` and `streamablehttp_client` must be importable from
      `mcp.client.streamable_http`.  `GetSessionIdCallback` must remain importable as well.
requirements.acceptance_tests:

requirements.description:
`mcp.types` does not export a name `CreateMessageResultWithTools`, causing an ImportError
      that blocks collection of:
        - tests/test_types.py (14 tests)
        - tests/client/test_sampling_callback.py (3 tests)
      The existing `CreateMessageResult` class already carries a `content_as_list` property.
      The tests require a separate exported name `CreateMessageResultWithTools` that:
        - is a Pydantic model supporting `model_validate`;
        - accepts the same fields as `CreateMessageResult` (role, content, model, stopReason);
        - allows `ToolUseContent` as the value of `content` and correctly round-trips it through
          `model_validate`;
        - exposes a `content_as_list` property returning
          `list[SamplingMessageContentBlock]`.
      Additionally, tests/client/test_sampling_callback.py::test_create_message_backwards_compat_single_content
      asserts that a plain `CreateMessageResult` instance does NOT expose a callable attribute
      named `content_as_list` (i.e., `content_as_list` either does not exist on
      `CreateMessageResult` instances, or is a property returning a non-callable value such as
      a list).  The current implementation already satisfies this constraint because
      `content_as_list` is a property returning a list; no change to `CreateMessageResult` is
      required unless tests indicate otherwise.
requirements.contract:
Add `CreateMessageResultWithTools` to `src/mcp/types.py` and ensure it is exported at
      module scope.

      Behavioural contract:
        - `CreateMessageResultWithTools.model_validate(data)` must succeed when `data["content"]`
          is a `ToolUseContent`-shaped dict (type="tool_use", name, id, input).
        - The resulting instance must satisfy `isinstance(result.content, ToolUseContent)`.
        - `result.content_as_list` must return a `list[SamplingMessageContentBlock]`:
            - When `content` is a single block, returns a one-element list containing that block.
            - When `content` is already a list, returns it as-is.
        - Fields: `role: Role`, `content: SamplingMessageContentBlock |
          list[SamplingMessageContentBlock]`, `model: str`, `stopReason: StopReason | None`.
        - The class must be constructable directly:
            CreateMessageResultWithTools(role=..., content=ToolUseContent(...), model=..., stopReason=...)
        - `CreateMessageResultWithTools` must be importable from `mcp.types`.

      Implementation note (behavioural, not prescriptive): the simplest conforming approach is
      either a TypeAlias of `CreateMessageResult` or a subclass that inherits all fields and
      the `content_as_list` property.  Either is acceptable as long as all acceptance criteria
      are met.

      - tests/test_types.py::test_client_capabilities_with_sampling_tools

requirements.description:
The module currently defines `JWTParameters` and `RFC7523OAuthClientProvider` but is missing

Add the following four public names to
      `src/mcp/client/auth/extensions/client_credentials.py`:

      ── `static_assertion_provider(token: str) -> Callable[[str], Awaitable[str]]` ──
        A module-level factory function.  Returns an async callable that accepts an `audience`
        string and always returns `token`, regardless of the audience value.

      ── `class SignedJWTParameters(BaseModel)` ──
        Pydantic model with at minimum these fields:
          - issuer: str
          - subject: str
          - signing_key: str
          - signing_algorithm: str  (default: "RS256")
          - lifetime_seconds: int   (default: 300)
          - additional_claims: dict[str, Any] | None  (default: None)
        Method: `create_assertion_provider(self) -> Callable[[str], Awaitable[str]]`
          Returns an async callable that, given an `audience` string, generates and returns a
          signed JWT with claims:
            iss=self.issuer, sub=self.subject, aud=audience,
            exp=now+self.lifetime_seconds, iat=now, jti=<uuid4-string>
          plus any entries from `self.additional_claims`.
          The JWT must be signed with `self.signing_key` using `self.signing_algorithm`.

      ── `class ClientCredentialsOAuthProvider(OAuthClientProvider)` ──
        Constructor signature:
          __init__(self, server_url: str, storage: TokenStorage,
                   client_id: str, client_secret: str,
                   scopes: str | None = None,
                   token_endpoint_auth_method: str = "client_secret_basic")
        The constructor must NOT require `client_metadata` or `redirect_handler` or
        `callback_handler` (unlike the base `OAuthClientProvider`).  Internally it may
        create suitable defaults.

        `_initialize(self) -> None` (async):
          Sets `self.context.client_info` to an `OAuthClientInformationFull` instance with:
            client_id=client_id, client_secret=client_secret,
            grant_types=["client_credentials"],
            token_endpoint_auth_method=token_endpoint_auth_method,
            scope=scopes (if provided).

        `_perform_authorization(self) -> httpx.Request` (async):
          Builds and returns an `httpx.Request` (POST) to the token endpoint with body:
            grant_type=client_credentials,
            scope=scopes (only if scopes was provided),
            resource=<server_url> (only when the current protocol version indicates it
            should be included, i.e., when
            `self.context.should_include_resource_param(self.context.protocol_version)`
            returns True).
          Client authentication is performed according to `token_endpoint_auth_method`:
            - "client_secret_basic": HTTP Basic auth header (client_id:client_secret).
            - "client_secret_post": client_id and client_secret as body params.

      ── `class PrivateKeyJWTOAuthProvider(OAuthClientProvider)` ──
        Constructor signature:
          __init__(self, server_url: str, storage: TokenStorage,
                   client_id: str,
                   assertion_provider: Callable[[str], Awaitable[str]],
                   scopes: str | None = None)
        The constructor must NOT require `client_metadata`, `redirect_handler`, or
        `callback_handler`.

        `_initialize(self) -> None` (async):
          Sets `self.context.client_info` to an `OAuthClientInformationFull` with:
            client_id=client_id,
            grant_types=["client_credentials"],
            token_endpoint_auth_method="private_key_jwt".

        `_perform_authorization(self) -> httpx.Request` (async):
          Determines the audience from the OAuth metadata issuer
          (`str(self.context.oauth_metadata.issuer)`).
          Calls `await self.assertion_provider(audience)` to get the JWT assertion string.
          Builds and returns an `httpx.Request` (POST) to the token endpoint with body:
            grant_type=client_credentials,
            client_assertion=<assertion>,
            client_assertion_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:client-assertion-type:jwt-bearer,
            scope=scopes (only if scopes was provided),
            resource=<server_url> (only when protocol version indicates it, same rule
            as ClientCredentialsOAuthProvider).

requirements.description:
The snippet demonstrates mounting a single StreamableHTTP server in an existing
      Starlette application, but it omits session-manager lifecycle management.
      When a FastMCP server is mounted as a sub-application, the parent Starlette app
      does not automatically invoke the session manager's startup/shutdown lifecycle
      (unlike when mcp.streamable_http_app() is used standalone).
      This means the example, as written, does not show production-ready usage.

      Additionally, because the file contains only 28 lines, the README code block it
      generates lands at lines 1388-1417 instead of the expected 1389-1428 (40 lines).
      The file must be extended to 38 lines of source content so that after README
      regeneration the block occupies lines 1389-1428.
requirements.contract:
The updated file must:
      1. Import contextlib (standard library) at the top of the import section.
      2. Define a lifespan async context manager function (using
         @contextlib.asynccontextmanager) that starts and stops the MCP server's
         session manager for the duration of the Starlette application's lifetime.
         For a single-server example, this means entering
         mcp.session_manager.run() as an async context.
         NOTE: mcp.session_manager is only accessible after mcp.streamable_http_app()
         has been called; structure the lifespan accordingly.
      3. Pass the lifespan function to the Starlette constructor via the
         lifespan= keyword argument.
      4. All other content (tool definition, Mount routing) must remain unchanged.
      5. The final file must be exactly 38 lines long (no trailing newline after the
         last line of code).
      6. The file must pass ruff check with the project's default configuration
         (line_length=120, target_version="py310").

requirements.description:
The snippet demonstrates mounting multiple StreamableHTTP servers under different
      paths, but omits session-manager lifecycle management for either server.
      Because two server session managers must both be started and stopped, the
      pattern requires an AsyncExitStack (as shown in
      examples/snippets/servers/streamable_starlette_mount.py).

      The file must be extended to 54 lines so that after README regeneration the
      code block lands at lines 1483-1538 (expected) instead of 1462-1504 (current).
requirements.contract:
The updated file must:
      1. Import contextlib at the top of the import section.
      2. Define a lifespan async context manager (using @contextlib.asynccontextmanager)
         that uses contextlib.AsyncExitStack to enter the session_manager.run() context
         for both api_mcp and chat_mcp.
         The lifespan function signature must accept an app parameter (Starlette instance).
         NOTE: each server's session_manager is only accessible after
         streamable_http_app() has been called for that server.
      3. Pass the lifespan function to the Starlette constructor.
      4. All other content (tool definitions, path configuration, Mount routing) must
         remain unchanged.
      5. The final file must be exactly 54 lines long.
      6. The file must pass ruff check with the project's default configuration.
